question: What is the difference between matplotlib and seaborn?

answers.answer: Matplotlib is for 2D plots only; seaborn is for 3D plots
answers.feedback: Not quite - both are primarily used for 2D plots. Matplotlib does have some 3D capabilities via mpl_toolkits, but that's not the key distinction.

answers.answer: Seaborn is built on top of matplotlib and provides a higher-level interface with better default styles and built-in support for pandas DataFrames
answers.feedback: Correct! Think of matplotlib as the engine and seaborn as a smarter dashboard on top. Seaborn handles a lot of the repetitive formatting for you and works naturally with DataFrames - you just point it at a column by name.

answers.answer: Matplotlib is free; seaborn requires a paid licence
answers.feedback: Both are completely free and open-source.

answers.answer: They are two names for the same library
answers.feedback: They are separate libraries, though seaborn depends on matplotlib under the hood. You often import both in the same project.

question: What is wrong with this code?
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bandgaps = [0.67, 1.12, 1.42, 3.37, 5.47]
melting_points = [938, 1414, 1357, 2830, 3550]

plt.scatter(bandgaps, melting_points)
plt.show()

answers.answer: Nothing is wrong - the code will produce a valid scatter plot
answers.feedback: The code runs without an error, but it's missing axis labels and a title. A plot without labels is not meaningful to a reader who doesn't already know what the data represents - always label your axes!

answers.answer: The code runs but is missing axis labels and a title, making the plot hard to interpret
answers.feedback: Correct! A complete plot should always include axis labels and ideally a title. Add these before plt.show(): plt.xlabel('Band gap (eV)'), plt.ylabel('Melting point (°C)'), plt.title('Band gap vs Melting point'). This matters even more when plots go into a report or a course book.

answers.answer: scatter() is not a valid matplotlib function
answers.feedback: plt.scatter() is a standard and commonly used matplotlib function for scatter plots.

answers.answer: Both lists must be the same type (e.g. both floats) to plot them
answers.feedback: matplotlib handles mixed numeric types fine - integers and floats can be plotted together without any conversion.

question: What does this seaborn code produce?
code:
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('materials.csv')
sns.boxplot(x='crystal_structure', y='bandgap', data=df)

answers.answer: A single box showing the distribution of all bandgap values
answers.feedback: Not quite - because x='crystal_structure' is specified, seaborn draws one box per crystal structure category, allowing direct comparison between groups.

answers.answer: One box per crystal structure, showing the distribution of bandgap values within each group
answers.feedback: Correct! This is where seaborn really shines - grouping and comparing distributions across categories takes one line. Each box shows the median, interquartile range, and outliers for that group. This is a powerful way to spot whether crystal structure influences bandgap.

answers.answer: A scatter plot of crystal structure vs bandgap
answers.feedback: sns.boxplot() specifically draws box plots. For a scatter plot you would use sns.scatterplot().

answers.answer: An error - seaborn cannot read directly from a DataFrame
answers.feedback: Working directly with DataFrames using column names is one of seaborn's biggest advantages over raw matplotlib.

question: You want to plot multiple graphs side by side to compare them. What does this code set up?
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].hist(df['bandgap'])
axes[1].scatter(df['bandgap'], df['melting_point'])
axes[2].boxplot(df['density'])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

answers.answer: Three separate plot windows, one for each chart
answers.feedback: plt.subplots() creates multiple panels inside a single figure, not separate windows. All three charts appear side by side in one image.

answers.answer: One figure with three plots arranged side by side
answers.feedback: Correct! plt.subplots(1, 3) means 1 row and 3 columns of panels. Each axes[i] is an individual panel you control independently. tight_layout() automatically adjusts spacing so the panels don't overlap. This is the standard way to present multiple views of a dataset together.

answers.answer: One plot that overlays all three chart types on top of each other
answers.feedback: Overlaying would require plotting on the same axes object. Here each chart gets its own separate axes, so they sit side by side.

answers.answer: An error - you cannot mix hist, scatter and boxplot in the same figure
answers.feedback: No restriction at all - each subplot panel is independent, so you can use any plot type in any panel.

question: A student wants to visualise the correlation between all numerical columns in a materials DataFrame. Which seaborn plot is designed exactly for this, and how is it called?

answers.answer: sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot=True) - displays pairwise correlations as a colour-coded grid
answers.feedback: Correct! df.corr() computes the correlation coefficient between every pair of numerical columns, and sns.heatmap() visualises the result as a colour grid. annot=True prints the numbers inside each cell. Values close to 1 or -1 indicate strong relationships - this plot is one of the first things an ML practitioner looks at before building a model, and it directly links to the multicollinearity topic coming up in a later lecture.

answers.answer: sns.scatterplot(data=df) - plots all columns against each other
answers.feedback: sns.scatterplot() needs specific x and y columns. To plot all pairs at once, sns.pairplot(df) does that - but for a compact correlation overview, the heatmap is cleaner.

answers.answer: sns.barplot(data=df.corr()) - shows each correlation as a bar
answers.feedback: A bar plot would only show one row of correlations at a time and is hard to read for a full correlation matrix. A heatmap is the standard tool for this.

answers.answer: sns.histplot(data=df.corr()) - shows the distribution of correlation values
answers.feedback: A histogram would tell you how many correlations fall in a certain range, but not which pairs of columns are correlated with each other. The heatmap preserves that information.
